<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2024]
**Define an aqueous two-phase system (ATPS). List the four main types of ATPS and state one characteristic application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
An ATPS is a liquid–liquid biphasic system in which both phases are predominantly aqueous (water content typically **>80% w/w**) and arise from the demixing of two structurally distinct water-soluble polymers, or a polymer and a kosmotropic salt, above a critical concentration. The four principal types are:
1. **Polymer–Polymer (PEG–Dextran):** high selectivity, used in lab-scale enzyme and nucleic acid purification.
2. **Polymer–Salt (PEG–phosphate):** cost-effective, used in industrial-scale enzyme recovery.
3. **Alcohol–Salt (Ethanol–ammonium sulphate):** low viscosity, used in bulk pre-purification of low-molecular-weight biomolecules.
4. **Ionic Liquid–Salt:** eco-friendly, used for thermostable enzymes and "green" processing.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2023]
**What is a partition coefficient in an ATPS? A protein has $K = 8.0$ in a system with $V_r = 0.5$. What is the percent recovery in the top phase?**

**Model Answer:**
The partition coefficient $K = C^{T} / C^{B}$ is the ratio of the equilibrium concentration of a solute in the top (PEG-rich) phase to that in the bottom (salt/Dextran-rich) phase. Recovery in the top phase is calculated as:

$$\eta = \frac{K V_r}{1 + K V_r} = \frac{8.0 \times 0.5}{1 + 8.0 \times 0.5} = \frac{4.0}{5.0} = 0.80 = 80\%$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A [Internal Choice Option 1] — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample, June 2024]**
> *(a)* Derive the expression for the recovery of a target biomolecule in the top phase of an aqueous two-phase system, starting from the definition of the partition coefficient. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* An enzyme has a partition coefficient of 5.0 in a PEG 6000 / phosphate system. The phase volume ratio is 1.5. A contaminant protein has a partition coefficient of 0.4 in the same system. Calculate: (i) recovery of enzyme in the top phase, (ii) recovery of contaminant in the top phase, (iii) selectivity, and (iv) one-step purification factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Definition of partition coefficient:**
$$K = \frac{C^{T}}{C^{B}}$$
where $C^{T}$ and $C^{B}$ are equilibrium concentrations in top and bottom phases.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Mass balance:**
$$m_{\text{total}} = C^{T} V^{T} + C^{B} V^{B}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Substitute $C^{T} = K C^{B}$ and $V^{T} = V_r V^{B}$:**
$$m_{\text{total}} = K C^{B} V_r V^{B} + C^{B} V^{B} = C^{B} V^{B} (1 + K V_r)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Recovery in top phase:**
$$\eta^{T} = \frac{m^{T}}{m_{\text{total}}} = \frac{K V_r V^{B} C^{B}}{C^{B} V^{B} (1 + K V_r)} = \frac{K V_r}{1 + K V_r}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Boundary analysis & significance:**
- $\eta^{T} \to 0$ as $K \to 0$
- $\eta^{T} \to 1$ as $K \to \infty$
- Tuning $V_r$ provides additional handle for optimisation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $K_{\text{enzyme}} = 5.0$, $K_{\text{contaminant}} = 0.4$, $V_r = 1.5$.

**(i) Recovery of enzyme in top phase (2 marks):**
$$\eta_{\text{enz}}^{T} = \frac{5.0 \times 1.5}{1 + 5.0 \times 1.5} = \frac{7.5}{8.5} = 0.8824 \approx 88.24\%$$

**(ii) Recovery of contaminant in top phase (2 marks):**
$$\eta_{\text{cont}}^{T} = \frac{0.4 \times 1.5}{1 + 0.4 \times 1.5} = \frac{0.6}{1.6} = 0.375 = 37.5\%$$

**(iii) Selectivity (1.5 marks):**
$$G = \frac{K_{\text{enzyme}}}{K_{\text{contaminant}}} = \frac{5.0}{0.4} = 12.5$$

**(iv) One-step purification factor (1.5 marks):**
$$P_f = G = 12.5 \text{-fold purification in a single ATPS step.}$$

**Verification of purity gain in top phase:**
- Initial protein fraction of enzyme in total protein = 0.5 (50%).
- Final protein fraction of enzyme in top phase = $\eta_{\text{enz}} / (\eta_{\text{enz}} + \eta_{\text{cont}} \times \text{mass ratio})$ — but the **selectivity number $G = 12.5$ is the canonical answer** expected by KTU examiners.

---

### QUESTION B [Internal Choice Option 2] — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample, Dec 2023]**
> *(a)* Explain the Albertsson model for partitioning of proteins in ATPS. Discuss the role of each term in the equation. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Compare PEG–Dextran, PEG–Salt, and Alcohol–Salt systems on the basis of selectivity, cost, scale-up potential, and effect on protein stability. Which system is most suitable for industrial enzyme recovery and why? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The **Albertsson equation** is an empirical linear free-energy relationship expressing $\log K$ as the sum of independent physicochemical contributions:

$$\log K = \log K_0 + \alpha \cdot \Delta\Pi + \beta \cdot \Delta q + \gamma \cdot \Delta \chi_H + \delta \cdot \Delta MW^{2/3}$$

| Term | Physical Interpretation | Discussion (Marks) |
|------|------------------------|--------------------|
| $K_0$ | Reference partition coefficient (≈ 1 in a symmetric system) | Baseline; sets intrinsic partitioning when all other terms are zero. (1 mark) |
| $\alpha \cdot \Delta\Pi$ | Electrochemical contribution | $\Delta\Pi$ is the electrochemical potential difference between phases; controlled by **salt choice and pH**. Drives charged proteins. (2 marks) |
| $\beta \cdot \Delta q$ | Hydrophobic contribution | $\Delta q$ is the exposed hydrophobic surface area of the protein. Membrane and lipid-anchored proteins are routed to the PEG-rich top phase. (2 marks) |
| $\gamma \cdot \Delta \chi_H$ | Polymer hydrophobicity term | $\Delta \chi_H$ depends on PEG concentration; tunes overall hydrophobic balance. (1 mark) |
| $\delta \cdot \Delta MW^{2/3}$ | Size-exclusion (steric) term | Large proteins are excluded from the polymer-rich top phase and migrate to the bottom. The $MW^{2/3}$ scaling reflects surface-area dominance. (1 mark) |

> The model is **additive and modular** — engineers can optimise each term independently to drive $K$ to a desired value.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

| Feature | PEG–Dextran | PEG–Salt | Alcohol–Salt |
|---------|:-----------:|:--------:|:------------:|
| Selectivity | **Very High (10–1000)** | Moderate (5–100) | Low (2–20) |
| Cost | Very high (Dextran ~$2000/kg) | Low (phosphate <$1/kg) | **Lowest** |
| Scale-up | Difficult (viscous, hard to recycle) | **Easy** | Easiest |
| Protein stability | **Excellent** (low ionic strength) | Good (high salt can denature sensitive proteins) | Moderate (alcohol can denature) |
| Phase separation time | Slow (viscous) | **Fast** | **Fastest** |
| Polymer/solvent recovery | Possible (ultrafiltration) | Phase discarded | Distillation |

**Conclusion (3 marks):** For **industrial enzyme recovery**, the **PEG–Salt (typically PEG 4000 / potassium phosphate)** system is the most suitable because:
1. **Cost** — Phosphate salts are ~1000× cheaper than Dextran, making bulk operations economical.
2. **Scale-up** — Low viscosity enables rapid phase separation in industrial centrifuges.
3. **Selectivity** — While not as high as PEG–Dextran, selectivity of 10–100 is more than sufficient for industrial recovery where subsequent polishing steps exist.
4. **Tunability** — pH, salt type, and PEG MW provide a wide design space.

PEG–Dextran is reserved for **high-value, lab-scale purifications** (e.g., therapeutic proteins in R&D), and Alcohol–Salt is used for **low-cost bulk pre-purification** of small molecules.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks in ATPS Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1 + K V_r$ denominator** in the recovery formula — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing selectivity $G$ with purification factor $P_f$** — they are equal in linear regime but distinct in general; the question wording dictates which to use.
> 3. **Not stating units or stating "K is dimensionless" without justification** — KTU examiners award 0.5 marks for explicit mention of dimensionless nature.
> 4. **Mixing up top and bottom phase identities** — always specify *which phase* you are computing recovery for. Use a clear diagram.
> 5. **Forgetting to discuss the role of salts and pH** in the Albertsson equation — these are worth 2 marks alone in a 7-mark question.
> 6. **Skipping the boundary-case sanity check** in derivations — KTU 2024 scheme examiners explicitly reward the step (0.5 mark).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **ATPS = two aqueous phases** with >80% water in each — gentle, bio-compatible.
- **Types:** PEG–Dextran, PEG–Salt, Alcohol–Salt, Ionic Liquid–Salt, Micellar.
- **Master equations to memorise:**
  - $K = C^{T}/C^{B}$
  - $\eta^{T} = K V_r / (1 + K V_r)$
  - $G = K_i / K_j$ (selectivity / single-step purification factor)
  - Albertsson: $\log K = \log K_0 + \alpha \Delta\Pi + \beta \Delta q + \gamma \Delta \chi_H + \delta \Delta MW^{2/3}$
- **Phase diagram essentials:** Binodal separates single-phase from two-phase region; longer tie-line ⇒ higher selectivity.
- **Operational levers** (in order of typical use): PEG concentration, PEG molecular weight, salt type & concentration, pH, temperature.
- **Industrial choice:** PEG–Salt (cost, scale-up); PEG–Dextran (selectivity, lab scale); Alcohol–Salt (lowest cost, bulk pre-purification).
- **Key limitation of ATPS:** Polymer removal from final product (requires downstream ultrafiltration / diafiltration).
- **Key advantage of ATPS over chromatography:** Continuous operation, easy scale-up, low pressure, low cost, and biocompatibility.
- **Always convert percentages to fractions** (or vice versa) in numerical problems — a $0.889$ vs $88.9\%$ slip is the most common arithmetic error.
- **Boundary check your final answers:** $\eta$ must lie in $[0, 1]$; $K$ typically lies in $[10^{-3}, 10^{3}]$; selectivity $G$ is usually $>1$ for a useful separation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Ways of Steering the Partitioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT745 - HIGH RESOLUTION SEPARATIONS IN BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Ways of Steering the Partitioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steering of Partitioning** is the deliberate, systematic manipulation of physicochemical parameters of a liquid–liquid (typically aqueous two-phase) system, or the modification of the target biomolecule, to shift the **partition coefficient (K)** of a solute in a controlled, predictable, and often amplified direction. The goal is to direct a target biomolecule (protein, enzyme, nucleic acid, organelle, cell) preferentially into one phase while contaminants remain in the other, thereby achieving high-resolution separation.

In mathematical form, the partition coefficient is defined as:

$$ K = \frac{C_{T}}{C_{B}} $$

where $C_{T}$ is the equilibrium concentration of the solute in the **Top (light) phase** and $C_{B}$ is its concentration in the **Bottom (heavy) phase** at thermodynamic equilibrium.

The "steering" implies a *volitional, externally imposed* change in K, as opposed to its passive observation. The Nernst-like distribution at constant T and P gives:

$$ \Delta G^{0}_{trans} = -RT \ln K $$

Any factor that changes $\Delta G^{0}_{trans}$ is a "steering wheel" for partitioning.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Floating Mango Story"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you have a bucket with two immiscible liquids — a thin layer of coconut oil floating on top of water. You drop a small mango into it. Where the mango ends up depends on:
> - The **density and viscosity** of the liquids (composition)
> - The **acidity** of the water (pH) — a sour mango behaves differently
> - **Dissolved salt** in the water (salting out) — salted water pushes things out
> - A **tethered string** attached to the mango (affinity ligand) — pulling it to one side
> - **Temperature** of the bucket (warm vs. cold)
>
> The mango's "partitioning" is steered by every one of these knobs. In biotechnology, the biomolecule is the "mango" and the aqueous two-phase system (ATPS) is the bucket.

### 1.3 The Six Canonical "Steering Wheels" in ATPS

| # | Steering Knob | Primary Effect on K |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Phase composition (polymer type, MW, concentration) | Alters phase hydrophobicity, free volume, exclusion volume |
| 2 | pH of the system | Modifies net charge on biomolecule and ionizable polymers |
| 3 | Temperature | Shifts phase diagram, alters K |
| 4 | Neutral salts (Hofmeister / lyotropic) | Creates electrochemical Δψ, salting-out effects |
| 5 | Affinity ligands coupled to one phase polymer | Massive, biospecific amplification of K |
| 6 | Charged / hydrophobic groups on phase polymers | Electrostatic or hydrophobic directional bias |

### 1.4 Industrial / Engineering Relevance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in biotechnology:** Steering the partitioning is the operational heart of **aqueous two-phase extraction (ATPE)**, **countercurrent chromatography (CCC)**, and **partition chromatography** — all classified as high-resolution separations by the KTU 2024 syllabus. In industry (e.g., recovery of insulin, monoclonal antibodies, plasmid DNA, virus-like particles, intracellular enzymes), controlled steering reduces the number of downstream steps, lowers cost-of-goods, and enables continuous processing in flow-through modes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Six Steering Strategies — Exhaustive Theoretical Breakdown

#### **Strategy 1: Phase-Composition Engineering**

The composition of an ATPS is described by the **binodal curve** in a phase diagram. Operating *above* the binodal gives two co-existing phases. The line connecting the compositions of the co-existing phases is the **tie line**, and its length is the **Tie-Line Length (TLL)**.

$$ TLL = \sqrt{(C_{P}^{T} - C_{P}^{B})^{2} + (C_{S}^{T} - C_{S}^{B})^{2}} $$

where $C_{P}$ and $C_{S}$ are polymer and salt concentrations in top (T) and bottom (B) phases.

- **Higher TLL** → phases more dissimilar → larger $\vert \ln K \vert$ → stronger steering.
- **Lower MW of PEG** (e.g., PEG 400 vs. PEG 8000) → smaller exclusion volume → biomolecules partition more into the PEG-rich top phase.
- **Higher MW of polymer** → bigger excluded-volume effect → proteins preferentially partition into the salt-rich / lower-MW-polymer phase.

#### **Strategy 2: pH Manipulation**

For an ionizable biomolecule, the net charge $z$ at a given pH is governed by Henderson–Hasselbalch. The partition coefficient then depends on charge through the Nernst-Planck relation (when an interfacial potential exists):

$$ \ln K = \ln K_{0} + \frac{zF \Delta \psi}{RT} $$

- Operate at **pH ≪ pI** → protein positively charged → if bottom phase has negative potential → K increases into bottom phase.
- Operate at **pH ≫ pI** → protein negatively charged → reverses the bias.

#### **Strategy 3: Temperature Control**

$$ \left(\frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \frac{\Delta H^{0}_{trans}}{RT^{2}} $$

If $\Delta H^{0}_{trans} > 0$ (endothermic transfer), raising T increases K. ATPSs are normally mildly endothermic, so cold-room operation often stabilizes labile biomolecules while still allowing efficient partitioning.

#### **Strategy 4: Neutral Salts & the Hofmeister Series**

Adding a neutral salt (e.g., K₂HPO₄, NaCl, (NH₄)₂SO₄) at constant TLL:

- Creates an **interfacial electrical potential** $\Delta \psi$ between phases due to unequal ion partitioning.
- Causes **salting-out** (chaotropic vs. kosmotropic) — the lyotropic / Hofmeister series ranks ions by their ability to salt-out proteins:

  $$\text{Salting-out power: } SO_{4}^{2-} > F^{-} > Cl^{-} > Br^{-} > NO_{3}^{-} > I^{-} > ClO_{4}^{-} \quad (\text{anions})$$

  $$\text{Cation effect is smaller: } Mg^{2+} > Ca^{2+} > Na^{+} \approx K^{+} > NH_{4}^{+}$$

- For a protein of charge z, in presence of salt, K shifts as:

$$ \ln K = \ln K_{0} + \frac{zF \Delta \psi_{salt}}{RT} $$

#### **Strategy 5: Affinity Partitioning (The Strongest Steering Wheel)**

A **ligand** (substrate analog, dye, metal chelate, antibody) is covalently coupled to one phase-forming polymer (typically PEG). When the target binds the ligand, its apparent K is amplified by the binding constant.

$$ K_{app} = K_{0} \cdot (1 + K_{b} \cdot [L]_{free}) $$

where $K_{b}$ is the biomolecule–ligand binding constant and $[L]_{free}$ is free ligand concentration in the target phase.

- Example: **PEG–Cibacron Blue** for dehydrogenase purification → partition coefficient amplified by **10²–10⁴ fold**.
- Example: **PEG–iminodiacetic acid–Ni²⁺** for His-tagged proteins.

#### **Strategy 6: Hydrophobic & Charge-Modified Phase Polymers**

- **PEG–palmitate / PEG–stearate** → drives membrane proteins and hydrophobic proteins into the PEG-rich top phase.
- **Charged PEG derivatives** (e.g., PEG–SO₃H, PEG–trilaurylamine) → create permanent interfacial potential for charge-based steering.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K = C_{T} / C_{B}$ | Partition coefficient | $C_{T}, C_{B}$ in mol/L or U/mL |
| 2 | $\Delta G^{0}_{trans} = -RT \ln K$ | Free energy of transfer | J·mol⁻¹, R = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| 3 | $\ln K = \ln K_{0} + (zF \Delta \psi)/(RT)$ | Nernst-Planck partitioning for charged species | F = **96,485 C·mol⁻¹**, Δψ in volts |
| 4 | $TLL = \sqrt{(\Delta C_{P})^{2} + (\Delta C_{S})^{2}}$ | Tie-line length (system dissimilarity) | % w/w |
| 5 | $K_{app} = K_{0}(1 + K_{b}[L]_{free})$ | Affinity partitioning amplification | $K_{b}$ in M⁻¹, [L] in M |
| 6 | $\partial \ln K / \partial T = \Delta H^{0}_{trans} / (RT^{2})$ | van 't Hoff temperature dependence | K⁻¹ |
| 7 | $R = \text{Recovery} = \frac{K}{K + (V_{B}/V_{T})} \cdot 100\%$ | % recovery in top phase | $V_{T}, V_{B}$ phase volumes |
| 8 | $P = \beta \cdot (K_{1}/K_{2})$ | Selectivity / purification factor | dimensionless |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These steering principles are exploited in:
> - **Industrial enzyme recovery** (e.g., pullulanase, penicillin acylase)
> - **Monoclonal antibody (mAb) capture** in PEG–citrate / PEG–phosphate systems
> - **Plasmid DNA purification** (PEG 6000 / (NH₄)₂SO₄)
> - **Virus-like particle (VLP) extraction** for vaccine manufacture
> - **Membrane protein solubilization** using PEG–detergent conjugates

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Nernst Distribution Law for Partitioning

Starting from the equality of chemical potentials of solute *i* in the two phases at equilibrium (T, P constant):

$$ \mu_{i}^{T} = \mu_{i}^{B} $$

The chemical potential in any phase for a non-ideal solution is:

$$ \mu_{i}^{\phi} = \mu_{i}^{0,\phi} + RT \ln a_{i}^{\phi} $$

where $a_{i}^{\phi} = \gamma_{i}^{\phi} \cdot C_{i}^{\phi}$ is the activity in phase $\phi$.

Substituting into the equilibrium condition:

$$ \mu_{i}^{0,T} + RT \ln(\gamma_{i}^{T} C_{i}^{T}) = \mu_{i}^{0,B} + RT \ln(\gamma_{i}^{B} C_{i}^{B}) $$

Rearranging:

$$ RT \ln \frac{C_{i}^{T}}{C_{i}^{B}} = \mu_{i}^{0,B} - \mu_{i}^{0,T} + RT \ln \frac{\gamma_{i}^{B}}{\gamma_{i}^{T}} $$

Defining the standard free energy of transfer:

$$ \Delta G^{0}_{trans} \equiv \mu_{i}^{0,B} - \mu_{i}^{0,T} $$

We obtain:

$$ \ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^{0}_{trans}}{RT} + \ln \frac{\gamma_{i}^{B}}{\gamma_{i}^{T}} $$

For **dilute solutions** where the activity coefficients approach unity in both phases ($\gamma_{i}^{T} \approx \gamma_{i}^{B} \approx 1$):

$$ \ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^{0}_{trans}}{RT} $$

which is identical to the standard result stated in Section 2.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inclusion of Electrical Potential (Nernst–Planck for Charged Species)

When an interfacial electrical potential $\Delta \psi = \psi^{T} - \psi^{B}$ exists (created by ion asymmetry, charged polymers, or salt), the electrochemical potential replaces the chemical potential:

$$ \tilde{\mu}_{i}^{\phi} = \mu_{i}^{0,\phi} + RT \ln a_{i}^{\phi} + z_{i}F\psi^{\phi} $$

Equating the electrochemical potentials at equilibrium:

$$ \mu_{i}^{0,T} + RT \ln a_{i}^{T} + z_{i}F\psi^{T} = \mu_{i}^{0,B} + RT \ln a_{i}^{B} + z_{i}F\psi^{B} $$

Solving for $\ln K$:

$$ \ln K = \ln \frac{a_{i}^{T}}{a_{i}^{B}} = -\frac{\Delta G^{0}_{trans}}{RT} - \frac{z_{i}F(\psi^{T} - \psi^{B})}{RT} $$

Recognizing $\Delta \psi = \psi^{T} - \psi^{B}$ and defining $K_{0} = \exp(-\Delta G^{0}_{trans} / RT)$ (the partition coefficient in absence of charge effect):

$$ \boxed{\;\ln K = \ln K_{0} - \frac{z_{i}F \Delta \psi}{RT}\;} $$

This is the **fundamental steering equation for charged biomolecules**. The sign of $z_{i}\Delta\psi$ determines whether K is increased or decreased.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Affinity Partitioning Amplification

For a biomolecule B that binds a ligand L in the top phase with intrinsic binding constant $K_{b}$:

$$ B + L \rightleftharpoons BL \quad ; \quad K_{b} = \frac{[BL]}{[B][L]} $$

The total biomolecule in the top phase is:

$$ C_{B}^{T} = [B]_{T} + [BL]_{T} = [B]_{T}(1 + K_{b}[L]_{T}) $$

The free biomolecule still obeys the intrinsic distribution, so:

$$ [B]_{T} = K_{0} [B]_{B} $$

The apparent partition coefficient becomes:

$$ K_{app} = \frac{C_{B}^{T}}{[B]_{B}} = K_{0}(1 + K_{b}[L]_{T}) $$

Taking logarithms:

$$ \ln K_{app} = \ln K_{0} + \ln(1 + K_{b}[L]_{T}) $$

For strong binding ($K_{b}[L]_{T} \gg 1$):

$$ \ln K_{app} \approx \ln K_{0} + \ln K_{b} + \ln [L]_{T} $$

This shows that **affinity partitioning adds the binding free energy to the transfer free energy**, amplifying K dramatically.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Recovery in the Top Phase

For a single-stage extraction with volume ratio $V_{R} = V_{T}/V_{B}$:

$$ R_{T} = \frac{\text{mass in top}}{\text{total mass}} = \frac{C_{T}V_{T}}{C_{T}V_{T} + C_{B}V_{B}} = \frac{K \cdot V_{T}}{K \cdot V_{T} + V_{B}} $$

$$ \boxed{\;R_{T} = \frac{K}{K + 1/V_{R}} = \frac{K \cdot V_{R}}{K \cdot V_{R} + 1}\;} $$

This shows that **steering K up** (e.g., by affinity ligands) improves recovery, and so does increasing the volume ratio of the target phase.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How Each Steering Knob Shifts K

| Knob | Mathematical Effect | Direction of K shift | Typical magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ PEG MW (top phase) | Larger exclusion volume | ↓ K (pushes protein out) | 2–10× |
| ↑ TLL | Greater phase dissimilarity | $\vert \log K \vert$ ↑ | 5–100× |
| pH away from pI (with Δψ ≠ 0) | Charge amplification via Nernst | K shifts by orders | 10–10³× |
| ↑ Kosmotropic salt (SO₄²⁻) | Salting out of protein into PEG-poor phase | ↓ K (or reversal) | 2–20× |
| Affinity ligand (PEG–dye) | Adds $-\Delta G_{bind}$ | ↑↑↑ K (selectively) | 10²–10⁴× |
| PEG–palmitate | Hydrophobic interaction | ↑ K for membrane proteins | 5–50× |
| Lower T (cold room) | Smaller phase volumes, ΔH effect | Mild | 1.5–3× |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define partition coefficient (K) and state the Nernst distribution law for biomolecule partitioning in an aqueous two-phase system.

**Model Answer:**

The partition coefficient K is the ratio of the equilibrium concentration of a solute in the top (light) phase to that in the bottom (heavy) phase:

$$ K = \frac{C_{T}}{C_{B}} $$

The Nernst distribution law states that at constant temperature and pressure, a solute distributes itself between two immiscible phases such that the ratio of its concentrations (or activities) in the two phases is a constant, provided the solute is in the same molecular form in both phases:

$$ K = \frac{a_{T}}{a_{B}} = \text{constant at fixed } T, P $$

For dilute solutions, activities can be replaced by concentrations. The associated free energy of transfer is $\Delta G^{0}_{trans} = -RT \ln K$. (3 marks)

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the Hofmeister (lyotropic) series? Mention its significance in steering biomolecule partitioning.

**Model Answer:**

The Hofmeister series ranks ions according to their ability to salt-out (precipitate) proteins from aqueous solution.

- **Anions (most to least salting-out):**
  $$ SO_{4}^{2-} > F^{-} > Cl^{-} > Br^{-} > NO_{3}^{-} > I^{-} > ClO_{4}^{-} $$
- **Cations (less dramatic effect):**
  $$ Mg^{2+} > Ca^{2+} > Na^{+} \approx K^{+} > NH_{4}^{+} $$

**Significance in steering:** Kosmotropic (strongly hydrated) anions like SO₄²⁻ are excluded from the protein surface, driving the protein into the PEG-rich top phase. They also create an interfacial electrical potential Δψ, which selectively steers charged biomolecules according to the Nernst–Planck equation. Thus, simply replacing NaCl with (NH₄)₂SO₄ can amplify K by 10–100× without changing the bulk composition. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Affinity & Physicochemical Steering**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the principle of **affinity partitioning** as a strategy to steer biomolecule partitioning. Derive the equation relating the apparent partition coefficient $K_{app}$ to the intrinsic partition coefficient $K_{0}$ and the binding constant $K_{b}$. Mention one industrial example.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe how **pH and neutral salts** can be used to steer the partitioning of a charged protein between two aqueous phases. Derive the Nernst–Planck equation for a charged solute and explain its physical meaning.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Affinity Partitioning — Step-by-Step Model Answer:**

- **Definition [1 mark]:** Affinity partitioning is a high-resolution technique in which a biospecific ligand (e.g., a dye, substrate analog, metal chelate, or antibody) is covalently coupled to one phase-forming polymer (typically PEG). The target biomolecule selectively binds the ligand, shifting its equilibrium partition coefficient strongly into the ligand-bearing phase.
- **Mechanism [2 marks]:** The ligand is chemically derivatized onto the phase-polymer (e.g., PEG–Cibacron Blue for dehydrogenases and kinases; PEG–iminodiacetic acid–Ni²⁺ for His-tagged proteins). On mixing, the target biomolecule binds the ligand in the PEG-rich phase. Contaminants do not bind and remain in the salt-rich phase, yielding high selectivity (β > 100 is common).
- **Derivation [3 marks]:** Let B be the target biomolecule, L the free ligand in the top phase, and $K_{b}$ the binding constant. We have:
  $$ B + L \rightleftharpoons BL \quad ; \quad K_{b} = \frac{[BL]}{[B][L]} $$
  The total biomolecule in the top phase is $C_{B}^{T} = [B]_{T} + [BL]_{T} = [B]_{T}(1 + K_{b}[L]_{T})$. The intrinsic partition coefficient still applies to the free biomolecule: $[B]_{T} = K_{0}[B]_{B}$. Therefore:
  $$ \boxed{\;K_{app} = \frac{C_{B}^{T}}{[B]_{B}} = K_{0}(1 + K_{b}[L]_{T})\;} $$
  For strong binding ($K_{b}[L]_{T} \gg 1$), $\ln K_{app} \approx \ln K_{0} + \ln K_{b} + \ln [L]_{T}$, showing the binding free energy adds directly to the transfer free energy.
- **Industrial example [1 mark]:** Recovery of **lactate dehydrogenase** from *Lactobacillus* crude extract using **PEG–Cibacron Blue F3GA** in a PEG 6000 / potassium phosphate system. Amplification of K by 200–500 fold; >90% yield and >5-fold purification in a single extraction stage.

**(b) pH and Neutral Salt Steering — Step-by-Step Model Answer:**

- **pH effect [1 mark]:** The net charge $z$ of a protein is a function of pH relative to its pI. At pH < pI, $z > 0$; at pH > pI, $z < 0$. When an interfacial electrical potential $\Delta\psi = \psi^{T} - \psi^{B}$ exists, the partition coefficient for a charged species is governed by the electrochemical potential.
- **Derivation of Nernst–Planck equation [3 marks]:** At equilibrium, $\tilde{\mu}_{i}^{T} = \tilde{\mu}_{i}^{B}$, where $\tilde{\mu}_{i}^{\phi} = \mu_{i}^{0,\phi} + RT\ln a_{i}^{\phi} + z_{i}F\psi^{\phi}$. Expanding:
  $$ \mu_{i}^{0,T} + RT\ln a_{i}^{T} + z_{i}F\psi^{T} = \mu_{i}^{0,B} + RT\ln a_{i}^{B} + z_{i}F\psi^{B} $$
  Rearranging and defining $K_{0} = \exp(-\Delta G^{0}_{trans}/RT)$:
  $$ \boxed{\;\ln K = \ln K_{0} - \frac{z_{i}F\Delta\psi}{RT}\;} $$
- **Physical meaning [1 mark]:** The term $-(z_{i}F\Delta\psi/RT)$ acts as a charge-bias correction. If $z_{i}$ and $\Delta\psi$ have the **same sign**, K is reduced; if **opposite signs**, K is amplified. By simply adjusting pH (changing $z_{i}$) or by adding salts (changing $\Delta\psi$), one can steer a protein into either phase.
- **Neutral salt effect [1 mark]:** Neutral salts (e.g., K₂HPO₄, NaCl) partition unequally between PEG and salt phases, creating a Donnan-like interfacial potential $\Delta\psi$. Kosmotropic anions (SO₄²⁻, F⁻) strongly favor the salt-rich bottom phase, making $\Delta\psi$ negative. This drives positively charged proteins (low pH) into the top phase and negatively charged proteins (high pH) into the bottom phase.
- **Practical note [1 mark]:** Typical salt-driven K shifts are 10–10³ fold. The Nernst–Planck relation is the cornerstone for *predictive* steering of charged biomolecules.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Phase-Composition & System Engineering Steering (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat phase diagram, explain the concept of **binodal curve and tie line** in an aqueous two-phase system (ATPS). Discuss how **tie-line length (TLL)**, polymer molecular weight, and system composition act as steering parameters for biomolecule partitioning.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain **hydrophobic interaction partitioning** and **charge-modified phase polymers** as steering strategies. Give one industrial application of each.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Binodal, Tie Line, and Composition Steering [7 Marks]:**

- **Binodal curve [1.5 marks]:** A plot of polymer concentration (e.g., PEG, % w/w) on the x-axis versus salt concentration (e.g., phosphate, % w/w) on the y-axis. Above the binodal, the system separates into two immiscible aqueous phases — a PEG-rich *top* phase and a salt-rich *bottom* phase. Below the binodal, the system is a single homogeneous phase.
- **Tie line [1.5 marks]:** A line connecting the compositions of the two co-existing phases (top and bottom) at equilibrium. The **tie-line length (TLL)** quantifies the dissimilarity between the two phases:
  $$ TLL = \sqrt{(C_{P}^{T} - C_{P}^{B})^{2} + (C_{S}^{T} - C_{S}^{B})^{2}} $$
- **TLL as a steering parameter [1 mark]:** Increasing TLL (moving further from the critical point) makes the phases more dissimilar in composition, free volume, hydrophobicity, and electrochemical potential. This amplifies the magnitude of $\ln K$ for any given biomolecule. A useful rule is $\log K \propto TLL$ for moderately sized proteins.
- **Polymer MW as a steering parameter [1 mark]:** Higher-MW PEG (e.g., PEG 8000) creates a larger excluded volume, pushing proteins into the lower-MW (salt-rich) phase. Lower-MW PEG (PEG 400, 600) yields smaller excluded volume and lets proteins partition into the PEG-rich phase. A typical strategy is to **screen** PEG 400, 1500, 4000, 8000 to find the optimal K.
- **System composition [1 mark]:** Replacing K₂HPO₄ with K₂SO₄ (more kosmotropic) increases salting-out and amplifies K. Replacing dextran with hydroxypropyl starch (cheaper) changes the phase polarities.
- **Conclusion [1 mark]:** Thus, by manipulating TLL, polymer MW, and salt type/amount — the *three composition knobs* — one can systematically steer the partition coefficient over 1–4 orders of magnitude.

**(b) Hydrophobic & Charge-Modified Partitioning [7 Marks]:**

- **Hydrophobic interaction partitioning [2.5 marks]:**
  - **Principle:** Hydrophobic ligands (fatty-acid esters, alkyl chains, detergents) are covalently coupled to one of the phase-forming polymers — typically PEG (e.g., PEG-palmitate, PEG-stearate, PEG-trilaurylamine).
  - **Mechanism:** The hydrophobic moiety creates a micro-environment in the PEG-rich top phase that mimics a hydrophobic surface. Hydrophobic proteins and **membrane proteins** (which normally partition poorly into PEG) are drawn into the top phase via van der Waals and hydrophobic interactions.
  - **Steering magnitude:** K can be amplified by 5–50× for strongly hydrophobic proteins.
  - **Industrial application:** Recovery of **intramembrane enzymes** such as **bacteriorhodopsin** from *Halobacterium salinarum* using PEG-palmitate in PEG 6000 / phosphate systems — the membrane protein is selectively extracted into the top phase, leaving water-soluble cytoplasmic contaminants in the bottom phase.
- **Charge-modified phase polymers [2.5 marks]:**
  - **Principle:** Polymers bearing fixed charges (e.g., DEAE-dextran, PEG-sulfonate, PEG-phosphate) are introduced. The unequal distribution of these charged polymers creates a permanent interfacial potential $\Delta\psi$.
  - **Mechanism:** Following the Nernst–Planck relation $\ln K = \ln K_{0} - zF\Delta\psi/RT$, a fixed $\Delta\psi$ selectively amplifies K for biomolecules of the appropriate charge sign and magnitude. The sign of $\Delta\psi$ can be flipped simply by switching the polymer (e.g., DEAE-dextran is positive; PEG-sulfonate is negative).
  - **Steering magnitude:** K shifts of 10–10³× are routine; the technique is especially powerful for **isoelectric point-based separations** of closely related proteins.
  - **Industrial application:** Separation of **hemoglobin variants** (HbA, HbA₂, HbF) from cord blood using charged-phase ATPS, exploiting the small differences in pI.
- **Summary [2 marks]:** Both hydrophobic and charge-modified strategies are powerful *chemical modifications of the phase system*; they complement pH/salt and affinity partitioning, and together constitute the full toolkit for steering biomolecule partitioning in high-resolution ATPS.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT write `K = C_B / C_T`.** KTU examiners consistently deduct 1 mark for inverted definition. The KTU standard is $K = C_{T}/C_{B}$.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse TLL with tie-line composition.** TLL is a *length* in the phase diagram, not the slope or composition itself.
> 3. **For affinity partitioning, do NOT forget the "intrinsic" part.** The full equation is $K_{app} = K_{0}(1 + K_{b}[L]_{T})$. Writing only $K_{app} = K_{b}[L]$ loses 2 marks.
> 4. **The Nernst–Planck equation sign convention is strict.** KTU expects $\ln K = \ln K_{0} - (zF\Delta\psi)/(RT)$ with $\Delta\psi = \psi^{T} - \psi^{B}$. Mixing up the sign loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Industrial examples MUST be specific.** Writing "for enzyme purification" without naming a specific protein/ligand is treated as vague and loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Units are not checked on symbolic answers, but numerical K must be dimensionless.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core definition:** Steering = deliberate manipulation of K via physicochemical, affinity, or system parameters.
- **Six steering knobs:** Composition, pH, T, salt (Hofmeister), affinity ligand, charged/hydrophobic polymers.
- **Nernst distribution law:** $K = C_{T}/C_{B}$; $\Delta G^{0}_{trans} = -RT \ln K$.
- **Nernst–Planck (charged species):** $\ln K = \ln K_{0} - zF\Delta\psi/RT$. **Sign is critical.**
- **TLL formula:** $TLL = \sqrt{(\Delta C_{P})^{2} + (\Delta C_{S})^{2}}$.
- **Affinity equation:** $K_{app} = K_{0}(1 + K_{b}[L]_{T})$.
- **Hofmeister anions (most → least salting-out):** SO₄²⁻ > F⁻ > Cl⁻ > Br⁻ > NO₃⁻ > I⁻ > ClO₄⁻.
- **PEG MW rule:** Higher MW → larger excluded volume → pushes protein *out* of PEG phase.
- **Recovery formula:** $R_{T} = K \cdot V_{R} / (K \cdot V_{R} + 1)$.
- **Industrial exemplars to remember:** Insulin / mAb in PEG-phosphate; plasmid DNA in PEG-(NH₄)₂SO₄; dehydrogenases in PEG-Cibacron Blue; membrane proteins in PEG-palmitate.
- **Constants:** R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹; F = 96,485 C·mol⁻¹.
- **Sign convention:** $\Delta\psi = \psi^{T} - \psi^{B}$; Nernst–Planck carries a **minus** sign.
- **Highest-impact steering knob:** **Affinity partitioning** (10²–10⁴× amplification).
- **Cleanest predictive steering knob:** **pH + salt** (Nernst–Planck framework).

---

topics.title: Large Scale Extractions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT745 - HIGH RESOLUTION SEPARATIONS IN BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Large Scale Extractions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Large Scale Extraction** in biotechnology refers to the **industrial-scale separation process** in which a target solute (bio-product) is selectively transferred from one phase (typically aqueous feed) into another immiscible phase (organic solvent or aqueous second phase) based on differential solubility, partition behavior, or selective affinity. The process is governed by the **Nernst Distribution Law** and is mathematically characterized by the **distribution coefficient (K_D)**, **separation factor (α)**, and **stage requirement** derived from the **Kremser Equation** and **McCabe–Thiele construction**.

In the context of **PEBBT745 (High Resolution Separations in Biotechnology)**, large scale extraction encompasses:

- **Liquid–Liquid Extraction (LLE)** – solvent extraction of antibiotics, amino acids, organic acids, and alkaloids.
- **Aqueous Two-Phase Extraction (ATPE)** – PEG/salt or PEG/dextran systems for proteins and enzymes.
- **Solid–Liquid Extraction (Leaching)** – extraction of bio-actives from plant biomass.
- **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE)** – using supercritical CO₂ for non-polar bioactives.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy – The "Coffee Cup" Model:**
> Imagine you have a cup of water contaminated with salt and sugar. You add **vegetable oil** (immiscible solvent). The hydrophobic (oil-loving) impurities dissolve into the oil phase, while sugar stays in water. If you drain the oil, you have purified water. **Large scale extraction is essentially this "oil-and-water" separation performed on thousands of liters, in multi-stage cascades, to achieve pharmaceutical-grade purity.**

Key operational parameters (highlighted as **bold** standard metrics):

- **Distribution Coefficient (K_D)** – Ratio of solute concentration in extract to raffinate at equilibrium (**dimensionless**).
- **Separation Factor (α)** – Ratio of K_D values of two competing solutes (**dimensionless**).
- **Stage Efficiency (E_M)** – Actual performance vs. ideal equilibrium stage (**%, typically 70–90%**).
- **Solvent-to-Feed Ratio (S/F)** – Volume of solvent per unit feed (**dimensionless**).
- **Number of Theoretical Stages (N)** – Required for target recovery (**integer**).
- **Recovery Yield (Y)** – Fraction of solute extracted (**%, 0–100%**).

### 1.3 Classification of Large Scale Extractions

| Type | Driving Principle | Typical Application |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Single-stage Batch | One equilibrium contact | Crude recovery, low-purity products |
| Multi-stage Cross-current | Fresh solvent each stage | Small molecules, antibiotics |
| Multi-stage Counter-current | Solvent flows opposite to feed | Industrial antibiotics, citric acid |
| Differential (Continuous) Contact | No discrete stages | High-volume processing |
| Aqueous Two-Phase | Polymer–salt incompatibility | Proteins, enzymes, plasmids |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Solvent Extraction

The operation rests on three foundational principles:

**Principle 1 – Nernst Distribution Law:**
At constant temperature, a solute distributes itself between two immiscible phases in a fixed ratio, provided its molecular state is the same in both phases.

**Principle 2 – Mass Balance (Conservation):**
The total moles of solute entering the extractor must equal the sum leaving in extract + raffinate + accumulated (steady-state: accumulation = 0).

**Principle 3 – Equilibrium Interfacial Transfer:**
Mass transfer across the interface stops when the chemical potential of the solute is equal in both phases.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

1. **Feed Preparation:** The aqueous broth (containing cells, debris, product) is clarified (centrifugation/microfiltration) to prevent emulsion formation.
2. **Phase Mixing:** Feed is contacted with an immiscible organic solvent in a **mixer** at controlled RPM (typically **300–600 RPM**) and residence time (**2–5 min**).
3. **Equilibrium Attainment:** Turbulent mixing maximizes interfacial area; mass transfer proceeds until partition equilibrium is achieved.
4. **Phase Separation:** The dispersion flows into a **settler/Decanter** where gravity resolves the phases (residence time **15–30 min**).
5. **Solvent Recovery:** The extract phase is distilled/evaporated to recover solvent (≥ **95% recovery** for economics) and yield crude product.
6. **Raffinate Polishing:** The aqueous raffinate may be re-extracted (stripping) to recover residual product or discharged.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for numerical questions on this topic. Memorize every row.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_D = \dfrac{C_E^{\star}}{C_R^{\star}}$ | Distribution coefficient (extract/raffinate at equilibrium) | Dimensionless; valid for **dilute solutions** |
| 2 | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{K_{D,i}}{K_{D,j}}$ | Separation factor between solutes $i$ and $j$ | $\alpha > 1$; ideally $\alpha \to \infty$ |
| 3 | $Y = \dfrac{K_D \cdot (S/F)}{1 + K_D \cdot (S/F)}$ | Single-stage fractional recovery (extract phase) | $0 \leq Y \leq 1$; $Y \to 1$ as $S/F \to \infty$ |
| 4 | $Y_{CS} = \dfrac{(K_D S/F)^N - 1}{(K_D S/F)^{N+1} - 1}$ | Counter-current recovery (Kremser eqn. for stripping section) | $N$ = number of stages |
| 5 | $Y_{CC,n} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{K_D (S/F)}{1 + K_D (S/F)}$ | Cross-current average recovery over $n$ stages | Used for batch cross-current |
| 6 | $\psi = \dfrac{K_D \cdot S}{F}$ | Extraction factor (often denoted $\varepsilon$) | $\psi > 1$ favored; $\psi = 1$ is pivotal |
| 7 | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{x_F}{x_N} \right) \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{\psi} \right) + \dfrac{1}{\psi} \right]}{\ln \psi}$ | Minimum theoretical stages (Kremser) | For $\psi \neq 1$ |
| 8 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{x_F - x_N}{(x_F/x_N)^{1/N} - 1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{K_D}$ | Minimum solvent ratio for given $N$ | Algebraic rearrangement |
| 9 | $\text{AU} = K_D \cdot \dfrac{S}{F}$ | "Absorption Unit" / stage capacity | Used in McCabe–Thiele x-y plot |
| 10 | $\dot{m} = k_L a (C^{\star} - C)$ | Interfacial mass transfer rate | $k_L a$ in s$^{-1}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Large scale extraction is the **workhorse downstream operation** in:
- **Antibiotic manufacturing** (Penicillin G into amyl/butyl acetate; recovery > **95%**).
- **Citric acid production** (recovered from fermentation broth using tri-n-butyl amine + tributyl phosphate).
- **Insulin purification** (aqueous two-phase with PEG 4000/phosphate).
- **Plant alkaloid extraction** (Morphine from poppy straw using lime + organic solvent).
- **Vitamin and essential oil recovery** (super-critical CO₂ extraction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry benchmark:** A single antibiotic plant processing **100 m³/day** of broth requires **3–5 mixer-settler stages** with overall stage efficiency of **80–85%**, achieving final purity of **>98%** after crystallization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Single-Stage Extraction Recovery Equation

**Statement to prove:** For one equilibrium contact, the fractional recovery into the extract phase is

$$
Y = \dfrac{K_D (S/F)}{1 + K_D (S/F)}
$$

**Step 1 — Define operating variables.**
Let $V_F$ = feed volume (L), $V_S$ = solvent volume (L), $x_F$ = initial solute concentration in feed (g/L), $x_R$ = raffinate concentration (g/L), $y_E$ = extract concentration (g/L).

**Step 2 — Total mass balance over the stage.**
The total moles (or mass) of solute is conserved:

$$
V_F \, x_F = V_F \, x_R + V_S \, y_E
$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium relation (Nernst's law).**
At the exit of the mixer-settler, the two phases leaving are in equilibrium:

$$
K_D = \dfrac{y_E}{x_R} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad y_E = K_D \, x_R
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute equilibrium into the mass balance.**

$$
V_F \, x_F = V_F \, x_R + V_S \, (K_D \, x_R)
$$

Factor out $x_R$ on the right-hand side:

$$
V_F \, x_F = x_R \, (V_F + K_D \, V_S)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x_R / x_F$ (raffinate ratio).**

$$
\dfrac{x_R}{x_F} = \dfrac{V_F}{V_F + K_D \, V_S}
$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of solvent-to-feed ratio $S/F$.**
Define $S/F = V_S / V_F$. Dividing numerator and denominator by $V_F$:

$$
\dfrac{x_R}{x_F} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_D \, (S/F)}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the fractional recovery into extract.**
Recovery $Y$ equals 1 minus the raffinate ratio (fraction remaining unextracted):

$$
Y = 1 - \dfrac{x_R}{x_F} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + K_D (S/F)} = \dfrac{K_D (S/F)}{1 + K_D (S/F)}
$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Kremser Counter-Current Recovery Equation

Consider $N$ ideal stages arranged in counter-current flow: solvent $S$ enters stage 1, feed $F$ enters stage $N$. Let $x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_N$ be raffinate compositions after each stage (with $x_0$ = feed entering stage $N$, $x_N$ = final raffinate leaving stage 1).

**Step 1 — Component mass balance over stage $k$.**
The solute entering with feed to stage $k$ equals solute leaving with raffinate from stage $k$ plus solute leaving with extract from stage $k$:

$$
F \, x_{k-1} + S \, y_{k+1} = F \, x_k + S \, y_k
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the equilibrium relation $y_k = K_D \, x_k$.**

$$
F \, x_{k-1} + S \, K_D \, x_{k+1} = F \, x_k + S \, K_D \, x_k
$$

Rearrange:

$$
F \, x_{k-1} = F \, x_k + S \, K_D \, (x_k - x_{k+1})
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce extraction factor $\psi = K_D \, S / F$.**

$$
x_{k-1} = x_k + \psi \, (x_k - x_{k+1}) = (1 + \psi) x_k - \psi \, x_{k+1}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize this is a first-order linear recurrence.**
Solving the recurrence with the boundary condition $y_{N+1} = 0$ (fresh solvent entering stage 1 contains no solute) yields:

$$
\dfrac{x_N}{x_0} = \dfrac{1}{(\psi)^{N+1} - 1} \left[ (\psi)^{N+1} - \psi \right]
$$

which simplifies (for $\psi \neq 1$) to:

$$
\dfrac{x_N}{x_0} = \dfrac{\psi - 1}{\psi^{N+1} - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Recovery into extract.**
Final recovery from feed:

$$
Y_{CC} = 1 - \dfrac{x_N}{x_0} = \dfrac{\psi^{N+1} - \psi}{\psi^{N+1} - 1} = \dfrac{\psi(\psi^{N} - 1)}{\psi^{N+1} - 1}
$$

For the special case $\psi = 1$, the limit as $\psi \to 1$ gives:

$$
Y_{CC} = \dfrac{N}{N+1}
$$

This is the **canonical Kremser equation**, universally used in KTU numerical problems. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Counter-Current)

**Given data:**
- $K_D = 2.5$ for product P between isobutanol and water.
- $S/F = 0.8$ (volumetric ratio).
- $N = 4$ theoretical stages.
- $x_F = 5.0$ g/L.

**Step 1 — Compute extraction factor.**

$$
\psi = K_D \cdot (S/F) = 2.5 \times 0.8 = 2.0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Kremser equation.**

$$
Y_{CC} = \dfrac{\psi^{N+1} - \psi}{\psi^{N+1} - 1}
$$

Compute powers:

$$
\psi^{N+1} = 2.0^{5} = 32
$$

$$
Y_{CC} = \dfrac{32 - 2}{32 - 1} = \dfrac{30}{31} \approx 0.9677
$$

**Step 3 — Final raffinate concentration.**

$$
x_N = x_F \, (1 - Y_{CC}) = 5.0 \times 0.0323 = 0.1616 \text{ g/L}
$$

**Step 4 — Concentration in extract leaving stage 1.**
By overall mass balance:

$$
y_1 = \dfrac{F \, x_F - F \, x_N}{S} = \dfrac{V_F(5.0 - 0.1616)}{V_S} = \dfrac{4.8384}{(V_S/V_F)} = \dfrac{4.8384}{0.8} = 6.048 \text{ g/L}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
A 4-stage counter-current cascade recovers **96.77%** of the product, vastly superior to a single stage (where $Y = 2.0/3.0 = 66.67\%$). This **30-percentage-point gain** demonstrates the engineering value of staging.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Current vs. Counter-Current Comparison

| Parameter | Single Stage | Cross-Current (n stages) | Counter-Current (n stages) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solvent usage | Low | High (fresh each stage) | Low |
| Recovery (same S/F) | Lowest | Medium | **Highest** |
| Capital cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Industrial use | Lab/screening | Bench-scale purification | **Production scale** |
| Kremser form | Eqn. (3) above | Eqn. (5) above | Eqn. (4) above |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Multi-Stage Counter-Current Extraction Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fermentation Broth Feed F] --> B{Stage N Mixer}
    B --> C{Stage N Settler}
    C --> D{Aqueous Raffinate xN}
    C --> E[Organic Extract yN]
    E --> F{Stage N-1 Mixer}
    F --> G{Stage N-1 Settler}
    G --> H[Organic Extract yN-1]
    G --> I[Aqueous Raffinate xN-1]
    I --> F
    H --> J{Stage N-2 Mixer}
    J --> K{Stage N-2 Settler}
    K --> L[Organic Extract yN-2]
    K --> M[Aqueous Raffinate xN-2]
    M --> J
    L --> N[...continuing stages...]
    N --> O{Stage 1 Mixer}
    O --> P{Stage 1 Settler}
    P --> Q[Final Organic Extract y1 - PRODUCT]
    P --> R[Recycle Solvent Stream S]
    R --> O
    Q --> S[Solvent Recovery Distillation]
    S --> T[Crude Product]
    S --> U[Recovered Solvent S recycle]
    U --> R
    D --> V[Wastewater Treatment]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Feed $F$ enters at **Stage N** (rightmost); solvent $S$ enters at **Stage 1** (leftmost).
- Each stage consists of a **mixer** (mass transfer) → **settler** (phase separation).
- Final loaded extract leaves at **Stage 1** for downstream solvent recovery.
- Spent raffinate leaves from **Stage N** for waste treatment.

### 4.2 Single-Stage Block Diagram (Process Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Clarified Broth Feed VF, xF] --> P2[Mixer M1]
    P3[Fresh Solvent VS] --> P2
    P2 --> P4{Phase Dispersion}
    P4 --> P5[Settler / Decanter]
    P5 --> P6[Aqueous Raffinate VR, xR]
    P5 --> P7[Loaded Extract VE, yE]
    P7 --> P8[Distillation Column]
    P8 --> P9[Crude Bio-product]
    P8 --> P10[Recovered Solvent - recycle to M1]
```

### 4.3 Equilibrium Distribution Curve (Conceptual McCabe–Thiele Plot)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph X_Axis["Raffinate Composition xR g per L"]
        A1[Origin 0]
        A2[xF point on diagonal]
        A3[xN final raffinate]
    end
    subgraph Y_Axis["Extract Composition yE g per L"]
        B1[Origin 0]
        B2[y1 point on equilibrium line]
        B3[yN extract from stage N]
    end
    A1 --- A2
    A2 --- A3
    B1 --- B2
    B2 --- B3
    Q1[Operating line slope S over F] --> Q2[Equilibrium line yE equals KD times xR]
    Q3[Number of stages determined by staircase construction between operating line and equilibrium line] --> Q4[Stages N read off at intersection points]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **McCabe–Thiele Step Rule:** The number of theoretical stages equals the number of "steps" drawn between the operating line (slope $= F/S$, intercept $y_{N+1} = 0$ for fresh solvent) and the equilibrium curve $y^{\star} = K_D \, x$, starting at $(x_F, y_1)$ and stepping down to $(x_N, y_{N+1})$.

### 4.4 Mixing and Settling Equipment Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MIX["Mixing Vessel"]
        MI1[Impeller Shaft]
        MI2[Turbine or Paddle Impeller]
        MI3[Baffles for Turbulence]
        MI4[Feed Inlet at Top]
        MI5[Solvent Inlet at Side]
    end
    MIX --> SETTLER
    subgraph SETTLER["Settling Vessel"]
        SV1[Dispersion Inlet at Center]
        SV2[Interface Level Control]
        SV3[Light Phase Overflow Weir]
        SV4[Heavy Phase Underflow]
        SV5[Residence Time 15 to 30 min]
    end
    SETTLER --> SV3
    SETTLER --> SV4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define distribution coefficient and separation factor. State their significance in selecting a solvent for large-scale extraction of an antibiotic.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Distribution coefficient $K_D$:** The ratio of the equilibrium concentration of a solute in the extract phase to that in the raffinate phase at a specified temperature. $K_D = C_E^{\star}/C_R^{\star}$, dimensionless. **(1 mark)**
- **Separation factor $\alpha$:** The ratio of distribution coefficients of two competing solutes; $\alpha_{ij} = K_{D,i}/K_{D,j}$. **(1 mark)**
- **Significance:** A solvent with **high $K_D$** for the desired antibiotic ensures **high recovery in fewer stages**, reducing capital and solvent costs. A **high $\alpha$** between the antibiotic and impurities ensures **selective separation** in a single extraction cascade. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between single-stage, cross-current, and counter-current extraction with respect to solvent consumption and recovery.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Single Stage | Cross-Current | Counter-Current |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solvent consumption | Lowest | **Highest** | Moderate |
| Recovery | Lowest | Medium | **Highest** |
| Stages required for target | Many | Many | **Few** |

For the same solvent-to-feed ratio, counter-current gives the **highest recovery with least solvent**, making it the **industrial standard**. **(1 mark for table; 2 marks for explanation)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the working of a **multi-stage counter-current liquid–liquid extraction cascade** used in antibiotic recovery. Explain the roles of the **mixer**, **settler**, and **solvent recovery unit**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Penicillin G is to be extracted from a clarified fermentation broth using **n-butyl acetate** at pH 2.0. The distribution coefficient $K_D = 35$. The feed contains **10 g/L** of Penicillin G. The solvent-to-feed ratio $(S/F) = 0.5$. Determine:
   - (i) The extraction factor $\psi$. 
   - (ii) The minimum number of theoretical stages required to achieve **99% recovery**.
   - (iii) The final raffinate concentration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Process Description (7 marks):**

1. **Feed inlet:** Clarified, cell-free broth enters the **last stage** (Stage N) of the cascade at controlled pH (acidic for penicillin: pH ≈ 2). **(1 mark)**
2. **Fresh solvent inlet:** Pure n-butyl acetate enters **Stage 1** (opposite end). **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Mixer:** Each stage contains an agitated vessel (impeller speed 300–500 RPM) where the two phases are dispersed as fine droplets. The high interfacial area allows rapid mass transfer until partition equilibrium is approached. **(1.5 marks)**
4. **Settler:** The dispersion overflows into a gravity settler (residence time 15–30 min). Light organic phase rises; heavy aqueous phase settles. Interface level is controlled by weir/boot. **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Counter-current flow:** Loaded solvent moves from stage N → N-1 → ... → 1, becoming progressively richer in penicillin. Aqueous raffinate moves in the opposite direction, becoming progressively leaner. **(1 mark)**
6. **Solvent recovery:** The loaded extract leaving Stage 1 is sent to a vacuum distillation column (T = 50°C, P = 50 mbar) where n-butyl acetate (bp 126°C) is recovered at >95% efficiency and recycled; the bottoms yield **crude Penicillin G**. **(1 mark)**
7. **Raffinate discharge:** Spent aqueous raffinate from Stage N goes to wastewater treatment. **(0.5 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $K_D = 35$, $S/F = 0.5$, $x_F = 10$ g/L, target recovery $Y = 0.99$.

**(i) Extraction factor (1 mark):**

$$
\psi = K_D \cdot \dfrac{S}{F} = 35 \times 0.5 = 17.5
$$

**(ii) Minimum number of stages (3 marks):**

Using Kremser equation $Y_{CC} = \dfrac{\psi^{N+1} - \psi}{\psi^{N+1} - 1} = 0.99$.

Let $u = \psi^{N+1}$. Then:

$$
\dfrac{u - 17.5}{u - 1} = 0.99 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad u - 17.5 = 0.99u - 0.99
$$

$$
0.01 \, u = 16.51 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad u = 1651
$$

$$
17.5^{N+1} = 1651 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad (N+1) \ln 17.5 = \ln 1651
$$

$$
N + 1 = \dfrac{\ln 1651}{\ln 17.5} = \dfrac{7.409}{2.862} = 2.589
$$

$$
N = 1.589 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N_{min} = 2 \text{ stages (round up to integer)}
$$

**(iii) Final raffinate concentration with $N = 2$ (3 marks):**

$$
\psi^{N+1} = 17.5^{3} = 5359.4
$$

$$
x_N = x_F \cdot \dfrac{\psi - 1}{\psi^{N+1} - 1} = 10 \times \dfrac{17.5 - 1}{5359.4 - 1} = 10 \times \dfrac{16.5}{5358.4} = 10 \times 0.00308 = 0.0308 \text{ g/L}
$$

**Verification of recovery:** $Y = (10 - 0.0308)/10 = 0.9969 = 99.69\%$ ✓ (exceeds 99% target).

**Final Answers:**
- $\psi = 17.5$
- $N_{min} = 2$ stages
- $x_N = 0.0308$ g/L
- Achieved recovery = **99.69%**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the **Kremser equation** for fractional recovery in an N-stage counter-current solvent extraction system. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical plant extracts **citric acid** from fermentation broth using **tributyl phosphate (TBP)** in kerosene. The distribution coefficient $K_D = 0.4$. The feed contains **80 g/L** of citric acid, and the solvent-to-feed ratio is **1.5**. Compare the recovery achievable with:
   - (i) A **single-stage** extraction.
   - (ii) A **three-stage cross-current** extraction (equal solvent split per stage).
   - (iii) A **three-stage counter-current** extraction.
   
   Comment on the trend and identify the most economical configuration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The Kremser equation is derived using:

1. **Assumptions:** Constant $K_D$ (dilute solution), constant $S/F$ per stage, isothermal operation, equilibrium attained at each stage, two completely immiscible phases, no chemical reaction. **(1 mark)**

2. **Stage $k$ mass balance:** $F x_{k-1} + S y_{k+1} = F x_k + S y_k$. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Substitute $y_k = K_D x_k$:** Leads to recurrence $x_{k-1} = (1+\psi)x_k - \psi x_{k+1}$. **(1.5 marks)**

4. **Solve recurrence with $y_{N+1} = 0$ boundary** (fresh solvent): $(x_N/x_0) = (\psi - 1)/(\psi^{N+1} - 1)$. **(2 marks)**

5. **Final recovery expression:** $Y_{CC} = 1 - (x_N/x_0) = (\psi^{N+1} - \psi)/(\psi^{N+1} - 1)$. **(1 mark)**

(Detailed algebraic steps shown in Section 3.2 above.)

**(b) Numerical Comparison (7 marks):**

**Given:** $K_D = 0.4$, $S/F = 1.5$, $x_F = 80$ g/L.

**(i) Single-stage recovery (2 marks):**

$$
\psi = K_D \cdot (S/F) = 0.4 \times 1.5 = 0.6
$$

$$
Y_{single} = \dfrac{\psi}{1 + \psi} = \dfrac{0.6}{1.6} = 0.375 = 37.5\%
$$

**(ii) Three-stage cross-current, equal solvent split (3 marks):**

Each stage uses $S_i/F = 1.5/3 = 0.5$, so $\psi_i = 0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.2$ per stage.

Remaining after each stage: $(1 - Y_i)$ where $Y_i = \psi_i/(1+\psi_i) = 0.2/1.2 = 0.1667$.

Fraction remaining after 3 stages: $(1 - 0.1667)^3 = (0.8333)^3 = 0.5787$.

$$
Y_{CC,n} = 1 - 0.5787 = 0.4213 = 42.13\%
$$

**(iii) Three-stage counter-current (2 marks):**

$$
\psi^{N+1} = 0.6^4 = 0.1296
$$

$$
Y_{CC} = \dfrac{0.1296 - 0.6}{0.1296 - 1} = \dfrac{-0.4704}{-0.8704} = 0.5405 = 54.05\%
$$

**Comparison Table & Discussion:**

| Configuration | Recovery (%) | Solvent Used | Equipment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single stage | 37.5 | Minimum | 1 mixer + 1 settler |
| Cross-current (3) | 42.13 | Total = $3 \times 0.5 = 1.5$ | 3 mixer-settlers |
| **Counter-current (3)** | **54.05** | **1.5 total** | **3 mixer-settlers** |

**Conclusion:** Counter-current is **most economical** — it provides the **highest recovery (54.05%)** using the **same total solvent** as cross-current but with **superior driving force** at every stage. Single stage is insufficient for industrial purity; cross-current wastes solvent in early stages (where driving force is wasted on already-dilute raffinate). **(Closing 1 mark.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert percentage to fraction** — Using $Y = 0.99$ correctly, but writing $Y = 99$ in subsequent equations causes wrong stage numbers.
> 2. **Mixing up Kremser formula** — Students confuse the **stripping section** form with the **extraction section** form. Memorize: $Y_{CC} = (\psi^{N+1} - \psi)/(\psi^{N+1} - 1)$ is for **counter-current with fresh solvent**.
> 3. **Wrong boundary condition in derivation** — Forgetting $y_{N+1} = 0$ (fresh solvent inlet) leads to an incorrect recurrence.
> 4. **Units confusion in $K_D$** — If $K_D$ is reported in L/L but solvent and feed are in kg, you **must** convert to consistent volumetric basis or mass basis.
> 5. **Not rounding up $N$** — If calculation gives $N = 1.58$, you **must** specify $N = 2$ integer stages; you cannot install 1.58 stages physically.
> 6. **Skipping the McCabe–Thiele alternative** — Many KTU questions accept graphical solution as full credit; failing to mention it loses a mark on "methodology" sub-parts.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Large Scale Extractions**
> 
> ✅ **Nernst Distribution Law:** $K_D = C_E^{\star}/C_R^{\star}$; valid in dilute regime, constant $T$.
> 
> ✅ **Separation factor $\alpha = K_{D,1}/K_{D,2}$:** Must be $> 1$ for feasible separation; higher = purer.
> 
> ✅ **Single-stage recovery:** $Y = \psi/(1+\psi)$, where $\psi = K_D (S/F)$.
> 
> ✅ **Cross-current $n$-stage recovery:** $Y_n = 1 - [1/(1+\psi_{\text{each}})]^n$.
> 
> ✅ **Counter-current $N$-stage (Kremser):** $Y_{CC} = (\psi^{N+1} - \psi)/(\psi^{N+1} - 1)$; special case $\psi = 1$ gives $Y = N/(N+1)$.
> 
> ✅ **McCabe–Thiele construction:** Operating line slope $= F/S$, intercept at origin for fresh solvent; equilibrium line $y^{\star} = K_D x$.
> 
> ✅ **Mineral/equipment facts:** Mixer 300–600 RPM, settler residence 15–30 min, stage efficiency 70–90%, solvent recovery >95%.
> 
> ✅ **Industrial examples:** Penicillin → amyl acetate; Citric acid → TBP; Vitamin E → supercritical CO₂; Insulin → PEG-phosphate ATPE.
> 
> ✅ **Design heuristic:** Always prefer **counter-current** for production scale; **cross-current** for analytical/lab work; **single-stage** only for screening.
> 
> ✅ **Recovery calculation shortcut:** When $\psi \gg 1$ (e.g., $\psi = 17.5$ for penicillin), very few stages (1–2) suffice.
> 
> ✅ **Economic constraint:** Solvent cost = (1 - solvent_recovery_fraction) × fresh_solvent_cost; aim for >95% solvent recycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT745 - HIGH RESOLUTION SEPARATIONS IN BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Protein Partitioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — NON-PROTEIN PARTITIONING IN AQUEOUS TWO-PHASE SYSTEMS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Protein Partitioning** is defined as the selective distribution of biomolecules and bioparticles **other than proteins** — such as nucleic acids, polysaccharides, lipids, membrane fragments, organelles, viruses, and whole cells — between the two immiscible aqueous phases of an Aqueous Two-Phase System (ATPS), governed by a balance of electrochemical, hydrophobic, conformational, and biospecific interaction forces.

The distribution is quantified by the **partition coefficient** $K$:

$$K = \frac{C_{\text{top}}}{C_{\text{bottom}}}$$

where $C_{\text{top}}$ and $C_{\text{bottom}}$ are the equilibrium concentrations (or activities) of the solute in the top and bottom phases, respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> A value $K = 1$ denotes uniform distribution; $K > 1$ indicates preference for the top (PEG-rich) phase; $K < 1$ indicates preference for the bottom (Dextran/salt-rich) phase. For non-protein solutes, $K$ commonly spans **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{3}$** depending on solute chemistry.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Salad Dressing" Principle (Made Smarter)

Imagine a bottle of vinaigrette: oil and vinegar separate because they don't mix, and any particle dropped inside drifts to whichever side it "prefers" — a small spice pellet sinks (vinegar), while a hydrophobic herb leaf floats (oil).

Aqueous Two-Phase Systems work like a **water-in-water vinaigrette**: instead of oil and vinegar, we have two **water-soluble polymers** (e.g., PEG and Dextran) that genuinely *refuse* to share the same watery neighborhood above a critical concentration. The result is two clear, biocompatible, mostly water-filled phases.

Now, instead of herbs, we drop in a **DNA strand, a virus particle, or a yeast cell**. Each one acts like a tiny diplomat — it sizes up the "neighborhood" of each phase (its charge, hydrophobicity, surface chemistry, and even biological recognition sites) and decides where to settle. That *decision*, expressed as the ratio of concentrations, is what we call **non-protein partitioning**.

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike protein partitioning (which is dominated by surface charge and hydration), non-protein partitioning is a **multifactorial phenomenon** where **hydrophobicity, molecular conformation, surface tension, membrane affinity, and biospecific recognition** play disproportionately large roles.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Non-Protein Solutes in ATPS

| Category | Examples | Dominant Partitioning Driver |
|---|---|---|
| **Nucleic acids** | Plasmid DNA, RNA, oligonucleotides | Charge, conformation, base composition |
| **Polysaccharides** | Starch, cellulose fragments, glycosaminoglycans | Hydrophobicity, MW, branching |
| **Lipids & membranes** | Phospholipids, membrane vesicles | Hydrophobic interaction, interfacial adsorption |
| **Organelles** | Mitochondria, chloroplasts, microsomes | Surface charge, density, hydrophobicity |
| **Viruses & phages** | Bacteriophages, plant viruses | Capsid charge, surface hydrophobicity |
| **Whole cells** | Bacteria, yeast, plant protoplasts | Cell wall chemistry, surface charge, hydrophobicity |
| **Small biomolecules** | Amino acids, antibiotics, dyes, pigments | Log P, charge, hydrogen bonding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 High-Yield Focus:** The syllabus specifically emphasizes nucleic acids (especially plasmid DNA), viruses, and whole cells as the three non-protein systems most frequently assessed. Memorize their dominant partitioning drivers using the table above.

---

## 1.3 Why Non-Protein Partitioning Differs from Protein Partitioning

1. **Size range is enormous** — from a 100 Da amino acid to a 5 μm plant protoplast (a 10⁸-fold mass range).
2. **Surface-to-volume ratio is non-linear** — small solutes sense bulk phase properties; large particles sense interfacial tension and electrical double-layer forces.
3. **Conformational flexibility** — DNA supercoiling, polysaccharide chain coiling, and membrane fluidity all alter $K$.
4. **Multivalent surface display** — cells, viruses, and organelles display hundreds to millions of identical surface groups, amplifying weak forces.
5. **Biological recognition** — affinity ligands on the cell/virus surface can trigger biospecific partitioning unattainable for proteins.

# 📘 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Six Driving Forces of Non-Protein Partitioning

### 🔹 Force 1 — Hydrophobic Interaction (Most Important for Lipids, Membranes, Hydrophobic Cells)

The top PEG-rich phase is more hydrophobic than the salt/polymer-rich bottom phase. Solutes with high **log P** (octanol-water partition coefficient) migrate upward.

> [!NOTE]
> Increasing the **PEG molecular weight** (e.g., from PEG 1450 to PEG 8000) **increases** the hydrophobicity of the top phase, pulling lipid and membrane solutes upward. This is the most routinely used lever in cell/organelle partitioning.

### 🔹 Force 2 — Electrochemical Potential (Donnan-type Distribution)

For charged non-protein solutes (DNA, acidic polysaccharides, charged viral capsids), the partition coefficient follows an electrochemical Boltzmann-like relation:

$$\ln K = \ln K_0 + \frac{Z \cdot F \cdot \Delta \psi}{R T}$$

where:
- $Z$ = net charge of the solute
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C·mol⁻¹**
- $\Delta \psi$ = interfacial Galvani potential difference (V)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $K_0$ = partition coefficient of an uncharged analog of similar size

### 🔹 Force 3 — Conformational / Entropic Effects (Critical for Nucleic Acids)

Double-stranded DNA, single-stranded DNA, and RNA have different flexibilities and effective hydrodynamic volumes. A more compact conformation exposes less surface to the solvent, altering $K$ by 2–10×.

$$\ln K_{\text{dsDNA}} - \ln K_{\text{ssDNA}} \approx \frac{\Delta G_{\text{conf}}}{RT}$$

### 🔹 Force 4 — Interfacial Adsorption (Dominant for Membranes, Viruses, Cells)

Large particles often concentrate at the **liquid–liquid interface** rather than in either bulk phase. This is captured by the **interfacial tension** $\gamma$ (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ mN·m⁻¹ in ATPS — extremely low!).

The probability of a spherical particle of radius $r$ residing at the interface is governed by:

$$\Delta G_{\text{ads}} = -\pi r^2 \gamma (1 - \cos \theta)^2$$

where $\theta$ is the contact angle (a measure of relative wettability of the two phases by the particle).

### 🔹 Force 5 — Affinity Partitioning (Engineered Selectivity)

A **ligand-PEG conjugate** (e.g., triazine dye-PEG, boronate-PEG, metal-chelate-PEG) is added to the system. Non-protein solutes with affinity for the ligand (DNA-binding dyes, glycoprotein-bearing viruses, lectin-displaying cells) are dragged preferentially into the PEG-rich top phase.

$$K_{\text{affinity}} = K_{\text{blank}} \cdot (1 + K_L \cdot [L])$$

where $K_L$ is the ligand-solute binding constant and $[L]$ is the free ligand-PEG concentration.

### 🔹 Force 6 — Density / Volume-Exclusion (Cells, Organelles)

The bottom phase (Dextran- or salt-rich) is denser ($\rho \approx 1.05$–$1.15$ g·mL⁻¹). Whole cells and dense organelles may settle there by **buoyancy/excluded-volume** effects.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Mandatory for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below must be memorized for KTU ESE — they appear in 8 out of 10 past year Module 2 numericals.**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K = C_{\text{top}} / C_{\text{bottom}}$ | Partition coefficient | dimensionless |
| 2 | $Y = \dfrac{K \cdot V_t}{K \cdot V_t + V_b} \times 100\%$ | % Recovery in top phase | $V_t$, $V_b$ in mL |
| 3 | $\text{PF} = K_{\text{target}} / K_{\text{impurity}}$ | Purification factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\ln K = \ln K_0 + \dfrac{Z F \Delta \psi}{RT}$ | Electrochemical partitioning | $F$ in C·mol⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\ln K = \alpha M + \beta$ | Albertsson empirical relation (polymer-salt systems) | $M$ = solute MW (Da) |
| 6 | $\ln K = \dfrac{M \lambda}{k_B T}$ | Brønsted equation (macromolecular partition) | $k_B$ = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J·K⁻¹** |
| 7 | $\Delta G_{\text{ads}} = -\pi r^2 \gamma (1 - \cos \theta)^2$ | Interfacial adsorption energy | $\gamma$ in N·m⁻¹ |
| 8 | $K_{\text{affinity}} = K_0 (1 + K_L [L])$ | Affinity-partition enhancement | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\log P = \log \left( \dfrac{[S]_{\text{octanol}}}{[S]_{\text{water}}} \right)$ | Hydrophobicity index | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_R = V_t + \dfrac{V_b}{K}$ | Retention volume (chromatographic analogy) | mL |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plasmid DNA purification** for gene therapy and DNA vaccines (replaces CsCl gradients).
- **Virus-like particle (VLP)** harvesting in vaccine manufacturing.
- **Continuous counter-current ATPS** for industrial-scale antibiotic extraction.
- **Biphasic bioreactor** systems where cells grow in one phase and product partitions to the other (in-situ recovery).

# 📘 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Electrochemical Partitioning Equation

**Starting premise:** A charged solute of valency $Z$ senses a potential difference $\Delta \psi$ across the interface. At equilibrium, the electrochemical potential $\tilde{\mu}$ is equal in both phases.

$$\tilde{\mu}_{\text{top}} = \tilde{\mu}_{\text{bottom}}$$

Expanding the electrochemical potential:

$$\mu_{\text{top}}^0 + RT \ln a_{\text{top}} + Z F \psi_{\text{top}} = \mu_{\text{bottom}}^0 + RT \ln a_{\text{bottom}} + Z F \psi_{\text{bottom}}$$

Define $\Delta \mu^0 = \mu_{\text{top}}^0 - \mu_{\text{bottom}}^0$ (standard-state contribution, independent of charge) and $\Delta \psi = \psi_{\text{top}} - \psi_{\text{bottom}}$.

Rearranging:

$$RT \ln \left( \frac{a_{\text{top}}}{a_{\text{bottom}}} \right) = -\Delta \mu^0 - Z F \Delta \psi$$

For dilute solutions, $a \approx C$, so $\ln(C_{\text{top}}/C_{\text{bottom}}) = \ln K$. Also, the uncharged analog of the same size partitions with $K_0$ such that $\ln K_0 = -\Delta \mu^0 / RT$. Substituting:

$$RT \ln K = RT \ln K_0 - Z F \Delta \psi$$

Dividing by $RT$:

$$\boxed{\;\ln K = \ln K_0 - \frac{Z F \Delta \psi}{RT}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sign convention: if $\Delta \psi > 0$ (top phase is more positive), a cation ($Z > 0$) is repelled → $K$ decreases; an anion ($Z < 0$) is attracted → $K$ increases. This is the **Donnan-like asymmetry** exploited in DNA purification.

### Worked Numerical Example

A 7.2 kb plasmid DNA has effective charge $Z = -120$ (phosphate backbone). The ATPS interfacial potential is $\Delta \psi = -2.5$ mV (top is negative relative to bottom). The uncharged analog of similar size gives $K_0 = 0.8$. At 298 K, compute $K$.

$$\ln K = \ln(0.8) - \frac{(-120)(96485)(-2.5 \times 10^{-3})}{(8.314)(298)}$$

Step 1: $\ln(0.8) = -0.2231$

Step 2: Numerator = $(-120)(96485)(-0.0025) = +28{,}945.5$ J·mol⁻¹

Step 3: Denominator = $8.314 \times 298 = 2477.572$ J·mol⁻¹

Step 4: Ratio = $28{,}945.5 / 2477.572 = 11.683$

Step 5: $\ln K = -0.2231 - 11.683 = -11.906$

Step 6: $K = e^{-11.906} = 6.71 \times 10^{-6}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The highly negatively charged plasmid is overwhelmingly repelled from the (also negative) top phase and concentrates 150,000-fold in the bottom phase. This is the molecular basis for the standard "**PEG top / salt bottom**" plasmid DNA purification protocol.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Brønsted Equation for Non-Protein Macromolecules

The Brønsted equation connects $K$ to the molecular weight $M$ of the partitioned solute in a polymer–polymer ATPS.

**Premise:** A macromolecule of mass $M$ entering a phase must displace an equivalent volume of solvent. The free-energy cost is proportional to $M$.

$$\Delta \mu = \mu_{\text{top}} - \mu_{\text{bottom}} = M \lambda$$

where $\lambda$ is a phase-system-specific constant (J·g⁻¹) reflecting the difference in solvent quality between the two phases.

At equilibrium:

$$\Delta \mu = RT \ln K = M \lambda$$

Dividing by Avogadro's number $N_A$ to switch from molar to molecular:

$$k_B T \ln K = m \lambda_{\text{mol}}$$

where $m = M / N_A$ is the molecular mass in kg. Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;\ln K = \frac{M \lambda}{R T}\;}$$

where $R = N_A k_B$ and $\lambda$ is now expressed per mole.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** The Brønsted equation assumes the solute has the same conformation in both phases. For flexible non-protein polymers (single-stranded DNA, branched polysaccharides), this assumption often fails, and an effective $M_{\text{eff}}$ must be used.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recovery and Purification Factor in Multi-Solute Systems

For a mixture of solutes (e.g., plasmid DNA + RNA + protein impurity), each with its own $K_i$ and volume $V_t$ (top) and $V_b$ (bottom):

**Recovery of solute $i$ in top phase:**

$$Y_i = \frac{K_i V_t}{K_i V_t + V_b}$$

**Purification factor of target $T$ over impurity $X$ in top phase:**

$$\text{PF} = \frac{C_T^{\text{top}} / C_T^{\text{feed}}}{C_X^{\text{top}} / C_X^{\text{feed}}} = \frac{Y_T / V_t}{(1 - Y_T) / V_b \cdot 0} \cdot \frac{Y_X / V_t}{...}$$

A more compact form:

$$\boxed{\;\text{PF} = \frac{K_T}{K_X} \cdot \frac{K_X V_t + V_b}{K_T V_t + V_b}\;}$$

### Worked Example

In a PEG 6000 / Dextran 500 ATPS with $V_t = 4$ mL and $V_b = 6$ mL:
- Plasmid DNA: $K_T = 0.05$
- RNA impurity: $K_X = 0.8$

Compute recovery and PF.

Step 1: $Y_T = (0.05 \times 4) / (0.05 \times 4 + 6) = 0.2 / 6.2 = 0.0323$ → **3.23%**

Step 2: $Y_X = (0.8 \times 4) / (0.8 \times 4 + 6) = 3.2 / 9.2 = 0.3478$ → **34.78%**

Step 3: $\text{PF} = (0.05 / 0.8) \times (0.8 \times 4 + 6) / (0.05 \times 4 + 6)$

Step 4: $\text{PF} = 0.0625 \times 9.2 / 6.2 = 0.0625 \times 1.4839 = 0.0927$

> [!WARNING]
> **A common student error:** Interpreting the equation $K = C_{\text{top}}/C_{\text{bottom}}$ as the **ratio of recovered amounts**. It is a **concentration ratio**, not a mass ratio. The volume terms $V_t$ and $V_b$ in the recovery formula are NOT inside the $K$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Solute Behavior Summary

| Solute Class | Typical $K$ Range | Best Phase Preference | Tactic to Improve Selectivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plasmid DNA (large) | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-2}$ | Bottom (salt-rich) | Increase $\Delta \psi$ (raise NaCl or pH) |
| RNA (small) | $10^{-1}$ – 1 | Slight bottom | Add RNase-free Mg²⁺; use pH 8–9 |
| Bacterial cells (E. coli) | 0.1 – 10 | Top (PEG-rich) | Increase PEG MW; add 0.1 M NaCl |
| Yeast cells | 5 – 100 | Top | Use PEG 8000 + Dextran T500 |
| Bacteriophages | 0.5 – 50 | Top | Affinity ligand-PEG (e.g., lectin-PEG) |
| Plant protoplasts | 0.01 – 1 | Bottom (denser phase) | Add sucrose to bottom phase |
| Mitochondria | 1 – 10 | Interface | Add Mg²⁺ to stabilize; low $\gamma$ |
